items.q: What happened in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.correct: There were two revolutions: February overthrew the Tsar and installed the Provisional Government; October saw the Bolsheviks seize power from that government.
items.misconception: The Bolsheviks overthrew the Tsar in 1917.
items.random_wrong: Lenin personally led the storming of the Winter Palace.
items.why: Students collapse 1917 into one event. The Tsar abdicated in February under pressure from liberals and soldiers — the Bolsheviks took power eight months later from a republican government.

items.q: Why were 19th-century industrial cities so unhealthy?
items.correct: Rapid population growth outpaced housing, water and sewage; lack of regulation produced overcrowded slums and cholera epidemics until reforms followed Chadwick's 1842 report.
items.misconception: Cities were unhealthy because Victorians did not care about hygiene.
items.random_wrong: Cities were unhealthy because factory smoke poisoned the food.
items.why: Victorians cared deeply but lacked germ theory and assumed disease came from bad air. The real problem was infrastructure, not indifference — leading to landmark public health acts.

items.q: Why did the Gunpowder Plotters try to blow up Parliament in 1605?
items.correct: Catholic gentlemen, frustrated that James I had not eased anti-Catholic laws as hoped, planned to kill the king and Lords and put a Catholic monarch on the throne.
items.misconception: Guy Fawkes planned and led the Gunpowder Plot.
items.random_wrong: The plot aimed to make England independent from the Pope.
items.why: The leader was Robert Catesby; Fawkes was the explosives expert. Students remember Fawkes because he was caught, but he was not the mastermind.

items.q: Who became British Prime Minister in 1940?
items.why: It's Winston Churchill. “Neville Chamberlain” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: German bombing of British cities. “A British attack plan” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did WW2 begin (Britain declared war in September of this year)?
items.why: The real figure is 1939. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
